page: The Vultr vc2-1c-1gb is an entry-level virtual machine belonging to the Cloud Compute server family. It features a single shared vCPU based on the x86_64 architecture, utilizing an Intel Core Processor from the Broadwell microarchitecture clocked at 2.0 GHz. The hardware configuration includes 1.0 GB of system memory and a local storage volume of 25 GB NVMe SSD. Network connectivity is supported by one complimentary public IPv4 address.

Performance benchmarks place this instance in the lower tier of cloud resources. The stress-ng single-core and multi-core CPU tests yield a score of 691.39, reflecting its shared-core allocation. Memory bandwidth is measured at 67.6 GB/sec for small block cached reads, dropping to 7.8 GB/sec for large block uncached reads and 5.19 GB/sec for large block writes. PassMark benchmarks show a CPU score of 1061.65 and a memory score of 683.72. Database operations and Redis SET performance are also positioned in the bottom decile, with Redis achieving 1.0 million operations per second and static web serving throughput reaching 0.95 GB/sec.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, this instance is designed as a highly economical option for low-resource environments where dedicated CPU performance is not required. The shared CPU allocation and minimal memory footprint make it unsuitable for heavy computational tasks, database hosting, or high-traffic applications. It is best suited for lightweight workloads such as basic static web serving, minor utility scripting, development sandboxes, or low-traffic testing environments.
description: The Vultr vc2-1c-1gb is an entry-level virtual machine in the Cloud Compute family, featuring a single shared x86_64 Intel Broadwell vCPU clocked at 2.0 GHz, 1.0 GB of RAM, and 25 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. Designed for budget-conscious deployments, its performance benchmarks—including a PassMark CPU score of 1061.65 and a static web serving throughput of 0.95 GB/sec—place it in the lower tier of cloud resources. This shared-core instance is highly cost-efficient for minimal resource demands but exhibits significant performance trade-offs under sustained loads. It is best suited for basic utility scripting, lightweight development sandboxes, and low-traffic static web serving.
